description: The image pixel is unreadable and no caption is provided. Based on the paper context, the figure likely displays engineering stress–strain curves or mechanical property summaries for low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel after cryogenic rolling (CR) to cumulative thickness reductions of 0%, 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90%. The extracted text reports that the undeformed sample exhibits a yield strength of 505 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 882 MPa, and elongation of 64%, whereas the 90% CR sample reaches 1791 MPa, 1861 MPa, and 6.6%, respectively. These data demonstrate a monotonic increase in strength and hardness from 284 HV to 578 HV alongside a drastic loss of ductility, evidencing a pronounced strength-ductility trade-off. Such mechanical evolution correlates with microstructural transitions from equiaxed austenite to elongated grains, dislocation entanglements, deformation twins, and eventually nanocrystalline fibers with an average grain size of approximately 58.8 nm. The figure presumably serves as direct quantitative evidence that severe cryogenic deformation can substantially strengthen lightweight austenitic steel through grain refinement and dislocation accumulation.

This schematic diagram illustrates the complete processing route for preparing cryogenically rolled Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel specimens. The upper left panel shows 100 mm × 100 mm billets being sectioned into plate-shaped specimens with thicknesses of 1.5 mm, 2.0 mm, 3.5 mm, and 10.0 mm. The upper middle graph depicts the solution treatment profile: heating to 1000 °C, holding for 1 h, followed by water quenching. The lower right subfigure shows the cryogenic treatment step where specimens are immersed in liquid nitrogen. The central and lower left panels detail the cryogenic rolling circulation process using working rolls; caption states that specimens were immersed in liquid nitrogen for 3–5 min, rapidly removed for a single rolling pass with about 2 % thickness reduction, and promptly re-immersed. These steps were repeated until cumulative thickness reductions of 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 90 % were achieved. The figure establishes the experimental framework demonstrating how severe plastic deformation at cryogenic temperatures is systematically imposed to investigate microstructure evolution and mechanical property changes in the austenitic steel.

Figure 2 comprises optical microscopy (OM) images capturing the microstructural evolution of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel across cryogenic rolling (CR) reductions from 0% to 90%. Subfigure (a) depicts the undeformed state with equiaxed austenite grains, straight grain boundaries, and sparse annealing twins; the accompanying text reports an average grain size of approximately 40.3 μm. Subfigure (b) (CR30%) shows grains beginning to elongate along the rolling direction (RD) with numerous intragranular slip bands and visible intersections of slip bands, signaling heterogeneous plastic deformation. Subfigure (c) (CR50%) and (d) (CR70%) display progressively flattened, banded, and elongated grains with increasing slip band density. Subfigure (e) (CR90%) exhibits a fully fibrous texture aligned along RD. Subfigure (f) (CR90%) provides a higher-magnification OM view (scale bar 1 μm) emphasizing intersecting slip bands. Together, these images document the systematic transition from equiaxed to fibrous austenite morphologies and escalating dislocation-mediated deformation features with increasing CR strain, underpinning the grain refinement and work-hardening behavior reported in the study.

This figure presents a series of TEM images documenting the progressive microstructure evolution of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel subjected to cryogenic rolling (CR) at thickness reductions of 0%, 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90%. Subfigure (a) shows the undeformed state characterized by sparse dislocation lines and distinct grain boundaries. Subfigure (b) at 30% CR reveals a Taylor lattice arising from the intersection of slip bands. Subfigure (c) at 50% CR exhibits dense dislocation walls alongside deformation twins. Subfigure (d) at 70% CR displays larger dislocation cells bounded by dislocation walls, with a reduced twin density. Subfigure (e) at 90% CR shows severely deformed fibrous grains; the inset SAED pattern exhibits continuous diffraction rings, indicating a randomly oriented nanocrystalline structure. Subfigure (f) is the corresponding dark-field TEM image of (e), revealing a nanocrystalline morphology with an average grain size of 58.8 nm according to the inset histogram. Subfigure (g) is a bright-field TEM image highlighting deformation twins persisting in the 90% CR sample. The overall sequence demonstrates a clear transition from initial dislocation lines to Taylor lattice, dislocation entanglements, deformation twins, dislocation cells, and ultimately nanocrystalline grains, accompanied by a reported dislocation density increase to 2.36 × 10^15 m^-2, providing direct structural evidence for the substantial strain hardening and strength enhancement observed in this steel.

Figure 4 presents X-ray diffraction characterization of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel subjected to cryogenic rolling at deformation levels of 0%, 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90%. Panel (a) shows XRD profiles where all specimens maintain a single-phase austenite structure with indexed γ(111), γ(200), γ(220), and γ(311) peaks, and no deformation-induced martensitic transformation occurs during rolling. With increasing deformation, the diffraction peaks exhibit progressive broadening. Panel (b) quantifies this trend via the full width at half maximum of the representative γ(220) peak, which rises monotonically from 0.496° in the undeformed state to 1.022° after 90% CR. Panel (c) presents the calculated microstrain and dislocation density derived from Williamson–Hall analysis: microstrain increases from 2.8×10⁻³ to 7×10⁻³, while dislocation density grows from 3.2×10¹⁴ m⁻² to 23.6×10¹⁴ m⁻². These data confirm that cryogenic rolling accumulates severe lattice distortion and dislocations while preserving phase stability.

Figure 5 presents EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and corresponding {100}, {110}, and {111} pole figures for low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel at three deformation stages: (a) undeformed, (b) cryogenically rolled (CR) to 30% reduction, and (c) CR to 90% reduction. Subfigure (a) displays equiaxed austenite grains with weak, nearly random texture intensities across the pole figures. Subfigure (b) shows progressively elongated grains and the emergence of the copper {112}<111> texture component, with pole figure maxima becoming visually discernible. Subfigure (c) reveals a fully fragmented, banded microstructure with austenite grains elongated along the rolling direction and a pronounced transition to the brass {110}<112> texture component, evidenced by sharply localized pole figure peaks. The systematic evolution from random orientations in (a) to strong deformation textures in (c) illustrates the strain-induced grain refinement and orientation fragmentation. The caption states that the fraction of low-angle grain boundaries increases substantially with deformation, reaching 89.4% at 30% CR and 94.1% at 90% CR, supporting the conclusion that cryogenic rolling progressively reconfigures grain boundaries and texture to underpin the observed strength-ductility trade-off.

Figure 6 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) grain boundary distribution maps and a corresponding misorientation angle frequency histogram documenting the microstructural evolution of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel subjected to cryogenic rolling (CR). In (a), the undeformed microstructure (scale bar 50 μm) consists of large equiaxed austenite grains delineated mainly by solid black high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs), with red low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs) being scarce. After 30% CR (b, 20 μm), LAGBs densely populate the interiors of elongated grains, signaling intensive dislocation activity and grain subdivision. At 90% CR (c, 5 μm), the structure transforms into heavily fragmented, rolling-direction-aligned bands with an extremely high density of LAGBs and only residual HAGBs. Panel (d) plots the misorientation distribution: the undeformed steel shows a dominant peak near 60° attributed to annealing twin boundaries, while the deformed states exhibit strong low-angle peaks; citation text reports LAGB fractions rise to 89.4% at CR 30% and 94.1% at CR 90%. These data demonstrate that progressive cryogenic deformation replaces HAGBs and twins with LAGB networks, driving the formation of ultrafine-grained banded structures that underpin grain refinement strengthening.

Figure 7 presents kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps derived from EBSD analysis for low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel subjected to different cryogenic rolling reductions. Panel (a) shows the undeformed state with equiaxed austenite grains exhibiting predominantly low KAM values (blue shades), indicating minimal local orientation deviation. Panel (b) for CR 30% reveals elevated KAM values (green to yellow and red) concentrated near prior grain boundaries, suggesting initial deformation localization. Panel (c) for CR 90% displays banded structures with significantly higher KAM values (red) within rolling bands and lower values (blue and green) in regions surrounded by low-angle grain boundaries, reflecting severe grain fragmentation. Panel (d) compares frequency distributions of average misorientation angles, showing a sharp peak near 0° for the undeformed sample, a broadened distribution centered around 1–2° for CR 30%, and a further shift toward higher angles for CR 90%. These results demonstrate progressively increasing geometrically necessary dislocation density and heterogeneous deformation with cryogenic rolling strain, supporting the microstructural evolution from equiaxed grains to fragmented banded structures.

Figure 8 presents the mechanical properties of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel after cryogenic rolling (CR) to 0% (undeformed), 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90% reduction. Panel (a) shows engineering stress–strain curves: the undeformed sample exhibits continuous yielding with low flow stress and exceptional elongation exceeding 60%, whereas CR-processed samples display markedly increased peak stresses and severely curtailed ductility, with the 90% CR curve reaching approximately 1861 MPa. Panel (b) quantifies these trends by plotting ultimate tensile strength (squares), yield strength (circles), elongation (red triangles), and microhardness (cyan stars) against deformation degree. Specifically, UTS rises from 882 to 1861 MPa, yield strength from 505 to 1791 MPa, and microhardness from 284 to 578 HV, while elongation plummets from 64.5% to 6.6%. These complementary panels directly evidence the pronounced strength–ductility trade-off in austenitic low-density steel under severe cryogenic plastic deformation, supporting the reported conclusion that the simultaneous increase in dislocation density, deformation twinning, and grain refinement drives the marked strengthening.

Figure 9 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of tensile fracture surfaces for low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C steel subjected to varying degrees of cryogenic rolling (CR), with a scale bar of 10 μm. Subfigure (a) shows the undeformed state exhibiting large, deep dimples characteristic of ductile fracture. Subfigures (b), (c), and (d) correspond to CR reductions of 30%, 50%, and 70%, respectively, displaying progressively refined and shallower dimple structures as deformation increases, indicating continued microvoid coalescence but with diminishing plasticity. Subfigure (e) for CR 90% reveals a markedly different morphology with flattened regions and partial cleavage features, signaling a transition toward a ductile-brittle mixed fracture mode. This systematic evolution from coarse dimples to fine dimples and eventual quasi-cleavage facets directly correlates with the reported strength-ductility trade-off, where ultimate tensile strength rises from 882 to 1861 MPa while elongation falls from 64% to 6.6%. These fracture morphologies confirm that increasing cryogenic deformation progressively refines the microstructure but compromises tensile ductility.
